speaker: interviewee
text: This is roll number 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry. You were saying about how the Russians were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three days we was with the Russians. We heard the horses. We heard the people. But we couldn't believe this was the Russians till the boy-- when the boy couldn't come over with a couple of potatoes or something. We didn't ask. We didn't [INAUDIBLE]. Till the boy one time came and said, uhm-hmm, and my brother went down, and he says, you know, why are you still laying here? The Russian is here. He couldn't believe. So then he started to go out. And the Russian saw us. And the Russian tried to see who are we hiding. We couldn't go. We went out like the mice went out. Dirty, of course. And they was looking and looking how we was living, how we went in and how we was there. They couldn't believe it. They was very sorry for us. And they tried-- and the Czech boy, the colony, they was afraid people should know they find us there. So they tried-- they want to take us with them. But we couldn't stand up. My both brothers, we didn't saw the light for years. So my both brothers, they was tall. They stand up, and they fell down. They stand up. And they were so sorry for, they couldn't-- with the beards. And so they gave us-- the [? colony ?] who my brother who was living before. So we went in to the place where my brother was living before. And everything was broke and this and this. But we was happy. And we found the people, the Czech people. They brought us bread and something clothing. And we was there two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, the Russian came, and the Nazis came. And the Russian came, and the Nazis came. And we was very afraid. So we went to [? Rivne. ?] In [? Rivne, ?] we was there a couple weeks. And every night, they  was bumbling. And every night was accidents. And every night-- my brother, the older brother, every night he wanted we should go out. We shouldn't be in this building. My younger brother says he don't care anymore. He don't want to run. The night before, before we left again for another place, these buildings got bombardiered, and this building fell down. We were lucky there wasn't people. And since then, we tried to survive. We tried. We went-- after this, we went to the same place where I was born in Dubno. My house where I was born was the Ukrainian people. They built up. They was looking because a lot of people was hiding things. Everything was upside down. And we went to look to my cousins. They didn't survive. There is different people. There is different. But my one cousin was-- the house was very removed from the war. So nobody was living there. So we went in there. And it was before Pesach. And we tried to bake matzah. Because like I said, my brother was working in a place where they're making flour and farina and this and this. So we got flour, and we got-- we made by ourselves matzah. We shared with all who survived. And this way, we started to live again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dubno?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slowly rebuilt your life there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in '45, we was afraid to be in Dubno. We wanted to immigrate to Israel or to America. So they said we have to go to Germany. And from Germany, we will  be able. So November in '45, I got married. And we went to Germany to Fernwald. Germany-- the place is Fernwald. There was my-- both children was born in Fernwald. Bernie was born in '46, 1946, November 1946. And Gail was born in 1950, May the 11th. My birthday is May the 10th. For my birthday, I got my daughter in 1950. Now, we have two married children and five grandchildren. And I got old and sick. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You eventually made it to Germany. Stayed there for how long before you were able to immigrate to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till '53. And then where did you go in 1953 when you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '53, we got a-- we bought a little farm. A very little farm. And at the farm, I raised my both children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Woodbine, New Jersey. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you raised your children and helped your husband with the farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, because we didn't believe to send away the children separate-- just to be with the children. And we didn't get many children. They always asked me. Everybody has grandparents. Everybody has uncles, aunts. And we don't have nobody. I didn't have nobody that should take care of the children. We didn't want to leave the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we bought a little-- a very little farm. And in 11 years, we paid off the farm. And the children got older. My son went  to high school. No, my son, he did college, and my daughter high school. And we remain in Florida until now. OK. When you came to America, did you face any discrimination? Or did you find that people were very welcoming to you when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the kind of people-- all the kind of people. Was very nice people, where they feel-- they felt sorry. But other people-- oh, where you get your shoes? Oh, where you get this? Where you get the dress? Where you get the coat? We was working very hard for our dollar. My husband, should rest in peace, he was working for $57. And in this year, for the $57, I saved up $1,000 with the $57, because I didn't bought the fresh bread. I was happy with the day-old [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We was living, and the children was very happy. We tried the best. But I was ironing. I was fixing stockings. I did what I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell your children what you and your first husband experienced during the war? Or did you keep that away from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I tried. I tried. They know. They belong to organizations. Unfortunately, I have plenty of problems. I have a daughter. She is now 45. And she's in a wheelchair with multiple sclerosis. My both children-- they are very happily married-- devoted families. My daughter has two sons. They both are in college. My son has three-- two daughters with a son and two children, they're in college. But unfortunately, my son is not a well person. He makes a nice living. But he is  a sick person. And my [? daughter-in-law ?] is in a wheel chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have a son-in-law, he's a  mother to my daughter. Do you know what they go through? In my old age, you know what I go through? Now, I'm very, very sick myself. And my daughter don't know. She knows I'm sick. But she don't know what's going  on with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to hide. But I will not be able to hide too much, because I'm going [INAUDIBLE] and I'm going straight to operation. So that's my life so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why was it-- or what message would you like to leave to someone watching this tape about what your life-- what you experienced during the war? What message would you like to tell someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: This should never happen again. We should never forget. We should teach our children. We should talk about this. It shouldn't happen again, because it is terrible, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish my children, and grandchildren, and all the Jews shouldn't go through this, what we went through. There should be peace in the world. One prayer I have should be peace of the world. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you for doing this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ester, if you could introduce this gentleman for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Abe Nisenholz. We know each other since we was in the camps. He kept a nice wife with two daughters. I got a husband with a daughter and a son. We was friends all the time in Fernwald. Later, we came to Philadelphia. He was in different business. We was in different business. But we came to each other. And unfortunately, his wife was-- got sick. My husband got sick. She died. And my husband died. And we find-- and 19 years later, we find each other in the organization by the survivors. And somebody came over and says to me, if I want to meet somebody, he came by himself. I came with my in-laws. So I say-- he says to the people-- but we was sitting at one table. He says to them he was looking for a nice woman that he could find, because he's by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody came over from the synagogue, what they know me, and says, somebody would like to meet you. I said, I have a home. If you think he's a nice man, this is my-- you have my phone number. Give him my phone number.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, this was-- this was October the 6th, '90. The next day, he came over. And he recognized my husband because of my dining room. I have a big portrait for me and for my husband. We didn't see each other 18, 19 years. He came over. And he says, I remember you. I remember your husband. We was friends. We came together from Fernwald. And this way we start together love. That is the-- that is our life story. And my children respect him very nice. His children respect me. The only thing-- we need to be healthy. We need to be well. That's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe who is in this picture, and when this was taken, and where this was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Fernwald in Germany. My husband made sodas, club soda, or seltzer. Here is my husband and my cousin. They both delivered the sodas to the little stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel Krik and [? Izaak ?] [? Gruber. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ester, when was this picture taken, and who's in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken-- where's the-- on the back is written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 15. '92.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '92.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my son and daughter-in-law, my son's two daughters, and this is-- and Justin. This is Bernie, [? Mimi ?], [? Nina, ?] [? Dena, ?] and Justin. Was his bar mitzvah. [? But ?] Justin's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ester, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is the bar mitzvah for my son's boy Justin. And this picture is my son-in-law Jeff, my daughter Gail, my grandson Mark, and my grandson Michael-- Gail's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ester, who is in this picture? In this picture, is Justin's bar mitzvah, me and my husband, and Allan, my husband's daughter, and my husband's son-in-law David.
